key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr-rich BCC/Ni-rich FCC interface with nanoscale chemistry in an ultrafine eutectic enables plastic co-deformation by promoting dislocation activity, twinning, and interfacial shear rather than brittle cracking.
engineering_relevance: Controlling interlamellar spacing and interface structure in additive-manufactured eutectics can improve the strength–ductility balance in high-strength structural alloys.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion to create an ultrafine Cr-Ni lamellar eutectic (~450 nm spacing)
Atomic-resolution STEM/HR-STEM to map interface structure, orientation relationship, and Ni segregation
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension, supported by TEM and atomistic modeling, to track deformation mechanisms
evidence_signal: In situ mechanical tests showed high flow stress with large plastic strains and no cracking, while correlative microscopy identified a sequence of interface-mediated plasticity, twinning, and mobile dislocation formation in the BCC phase.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated in a specific additively manufactured Cr-Ni eutectic, so translation to other alloys will depend on controlling interface chemistry, spacing, and thermal history.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
